conventions.text: Never use `any` — always use concrete types or `unknown` with a type guard
All `async` functions must wrap `await` calls in try/catch; never let rejections propagate unhandled
Custom hooks must start with the `use` prefix to satisfy React's Rules of Hooks
Remove all `console.log` / `console.error` before merging; use the `logger` utility instead
Component props interfaces must use the `Props` suffix (e.g. `ButtonProps`, not `IButton`)
`useEffect` dependency arrays must be exhaustive — do not suppress the exhaustive-deps ESLint rule
Only route page files should use `export default`; all other modules should use named exports
Avoid inline arrow functions as JSX event handlers in render — extract them as named callbacks

observations.text: TypeScript strictness is treated as a first-class quality gate. `any` annotations are blocked in 100% of reviewed PRs — no exceptions have been accepted.
Error handling standards tightened noticeably after a production incident in Q3 2024. Since then, async functions without explicit error handling have been blocked consistently.
React hook naming violations are becoming more frequent as the codebase grows. The pattern has appeared across 4 PRs from 3 different authors in the last 30 days — possibly worth automating.
Debug `console.log` statements appear in nearly every PR review. The team has discussed adding an ESLint rule but hasn't done so yet.

styleGuide: Annotate public API function signatures explicitly — don't rely on TypeScript inference for exported types
Prefer `unknown` over `any` for untrusted external data; narrow with runtime type guards before use
Custom hooks encapsulate all related side effects; colocate `useState` + `useEffect` inside the hook, not in the component
Errors propagate via returned state (`{ data, error }`), never by throwing from hooks into component render
Use `logger.info()` / `logger.error()` for all runtime output — no bare `console.*` in committed code
